questions.question: What is the key principle behind syndrome decoding?

options.text: The syndrome of a codeword is always the same
The syndrome of received word equals the syndrome of the error pattern
The syndrome indicates the message content

questions.explanation: Since syndrome(codeword) = 0, we have syndrome(r) = syndrome(c ⊕ e) = syndrome(e). The syndrome only depends on the error, not on which codeword was sent.

questions.question: What is a coset leader in coding theory?

options.text: The first codeword in the code
The minimum weight word in a coset (words with same syndrome)

questions.explanation: A coset is the set of all words with the same syndrome. The coset leader is the minimum weight element, representing the most likely error pattern for that syndrome.

questions.question: What is the time complexity of maximum likelihood decoding for an [n, k] code?
questions.explanation: ML decoding must compare the received word to all 2^k codewords, each comparison taking O(n) time. This gives O(2^k × n) total complexity.

questions.question: How many distinct syndromes exist for an [n, k] linear code?
questions.explanation: The syndrome is an (n-k)-bit vector, so there are 2^(n-k) possible syndromes. Each syndrome corresponds to one coset of the code.

questions.question: What is the covering radius of a code?

options.text: The maximum weight of any coset leader

questions.explanation: The covering radius is the maximum weight among all coset leaders, which represents the maximum number of errors that could occur in the worst case while still being in a coset with a defined correction.

questions.question: For the binary symmetric channel with error probability p < 0.5, what does maximum likelihood decoding minimize?

options.text: The Hamming distance between received and decoded words

questions.explanation: For BSC with p < 0.5, ML decoding finds the codeword with minimum Hamming distance to the received word. This minimizes the number of assumed bit flips.

questions.question: Syndrome decoding requires storing all 2^n possible received words.
questions.explanation: False. Syndrome decoding only stores 2^(n-k) syndrome-to-error mappings (one per coset). This is much smaller than 2^n for useful codes where k > 0.
